did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used machine learning (specifically XG-boost) to classify skin cancer (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) using pre-extracted features from the PH2 dataset. It also employed explainable AI (XAI) methods such as SHAP, partial dependence plots, and permutation importance to interpret the model results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing a machine learning model (XG-boost) for skin cancer classification using the PH2 dataset. It includes specific sections for methods, results (94% accuracy, 0.9947 AUC), and discussion of explainable AI techniques (SHAP, partial dependence plots), rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin cancer classification using explainable artificial intelligence on pre-extracted image features
brief_summary: This study developed a machine learning model to classify skin cancer as melanoma or non-melanoma using pre-extracted features from the publicly available PH2 dataset. The researchers employed explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) techniques, including SHAP, partial dependence plots, and permutation importance, to interpret the model's decisions. Among several algorithms tested, XG-boost demonstrated superior performance, achieving an accuracy of 94% and an area under the curve (AUC) of 0.9947. The XAI analysis revealed that asymmetry and pigment network features were the most influential factors in distinguishing between malignant and benign lesions. These findings indicate that utilizing preprocessed features enhances both the accuracy and interpretability of skin cancer classification models.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two primary classes in the binary classification task (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) using the PH2 dataset. The study focuses on distinguishing melanoma from non-melanoma skin cancer.
The comparator class in the binary classification task. The PH2 dataset contains images of melanoma and non-melanoma lesions. The study classifies these two categories.

Mentioned in the Introduction as one of the most frequent types of skin cancer alongside melanoma and squamous cell carcinoma, but not used as a specific label in the experimental classification task.
Mentioned in the Introduction as one of the most frequent types of skin cancer, but not used as a specific label in the experimental classification task.

Mentioned in the context of the PH2 dataset's original three-class structure (typical nevus, atypical nevus, and melanoma) in a related work citation (Alkarakatly et al., 2020) and in a description of the PH2 dataset's classes. However, the current study's experiment is described as a binary classification of 'melanoma vs. non-melanoma'.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin cancer as melanoma or non-melanoma
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the aim is to 'classify skin cancer' and 'distinguishes between non-melanoma and melanoma skin cancer' using the PH2 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) rather than continuous values. Performance metrics reported include accuracy (94%) and AUC (0.9947), which are standard for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: PH2 dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions. The original dataset contains three classes: typical nevus, atypical nevus, and melanoma. In this study, it was used as the source for pre-extracted image features.

use.training: Used to provide pre-extracted image features for training the XG-boost and other machine learning models.
use.testing: Used to provide pre-extracted image features for testing the models.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To estimate input feature importance and identify the strongest relationship between features and the target variable.
To find the relation between inputs themselves and between inputs and outputs, and to check for multicollinearity.
To determine feature importance by shuffling feature values and monitoring the change in model prediction error.
To display the marginal effect of a predictor variable on the target variable and to spot interactions or non-linear correlations.
To provide local explanations for specific predictions by localizing the model and perturbing input data to observe the impact of features on the outcome.

statistical_methods_used.method: Area Under the Curve (AUC) of ROC
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the model's ability to differentiate between positive and negative classes (discriminatory power).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing with p-values or significance tests. It utilized chi-square scores for feature importance estimation and correlation matrices for descriptive analysis, but did not perform statistical hypothesis tests to compare model performances or validate findings with significance levels.

PH2 dataset.notes: The study utilized pre-extracted image features from the PH2 dataset for binary classification of melanoma vs. non-melanoma. XG-boost and Decision Tree achieved the highest accuracy of 94%. The paper notes that XG-boost yielded an AUC of 0.9947. Other algorithms tested included KNN, SVM, and ANN. The study also employed XAI methods (SHAP, PDP, Permutation Importance) which identified asymmetry and pigment network as the most important features for distinguishing between melanoma and nevus classes.

architecture_details.description: The study utilized classical ML algorithms including KNN, XG-boost, decision tree, and random forest. XG-boost was identified as the primary model yielding superior performance. The model was trained on pre-extracted image features rather than raw images.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Ensemble stacking of ML models and deep learning
preprocessing_techniques: Feature extraction from preprocessed images (the study utilizes pre-extracted image features from the PH2 dataset rather than raw images; specific preprocessing steps applied to the images prior to feature extraction are not detailed in the provided text, only that features were extracted from preprocessed images)
Dimensionality reduction (implied by the use of pre-extracted features to reduce input data dimensionality)
conclusions: Utilizing pre-extracted features from preprocessed images in the PH2 dataset improves both the accuracy and interpretability of machine learning models for skin cancer classification compared to using raw image data.

The XG-boost algorithm demonstrated superior performance among the tested machine learning models, achieving an accuracy of 94% and an area under the curve (AUC) of 0.9947 for distinguishing between melanoma and non-melanoma skin cancer.

Explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) techniques, including SHAP, partial dependence plots, and permutation importance, effectively identified asymmetry and pigment network features as the most influential factors in distinguishing between malignant and benign skin lesions.

The proposed approach provides a computationally effective and objective method for skin cancer classification, addressing the subjectivity and variability associated with traditional human visual inspection and the ABCD criterion.

The study confirms that machine learning models trained on specific, pre-extracted morphological features can accurately classify skin cancer, with XG-boost outperforming other algorithms such as Decision Tree, KNN, SVM, and ANN.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the PH2 dataset, which is a small, publicly available dataset with limited sample size, raising concerns about generalizability and external validation.
The model utilizes pre-extracted image features rather than raw images, which limits the ability to assess the impact of different preprocessing pipelines or raw image-based deep learning approaches.

The binary classification task (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) simplifies the clinical reality by grouping distinct non-melanoma types (e.g., basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, various nevi) into a single class, potentially masking specific diagnostic challenges.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of the performance differences between models or feature importance.
The paper does not report sensitivity, specificity, precision, or recall metrics, relying solely on accuracy and AUC, which may obscure class imbalance issues if present.

The study does not address potential data leakage risks associated with the use of pre-extracted features, as the source of feature extraction and any potential overlap with training/test splits in the original PH2 dataset processing are not fully detailed.

Clinical relevance is limited by the lack of comparison with dermatologist performance or real-world clinical settings, making it difficult to assess the practical utility of the proposed XAI explanations.
